title: The Macro Minute: Are markets priced for change at the Fed? Should 401ks include alternative assets?

Happy Friday out there team 42 skipper
here Darius Dell to present our macro
minute for Friday August 8th 2025. Hope
everyone's having a great week. So as
always we'll start with our executive
summary from today's leadoff morning
note. If you would like to review the
analysis supporting these conclusions
and to see if you need to make any
changes in your portfolio to remain on
the right side of market risk you need
to be a client of 42 macro. So today's
macro questions are, are markets
adequately priced for regime change at
the Fed, part four, and should 401ks be
exposed to alternative assets? Our
answers, our 2-year-old forecast for
muchneeded regime change at the Federal
Reserve was incrementally ratified after
two developments yesterday. And no,
markets are not adequately priced for
this risk. First, Bloomberg reported
that Fed Governor Wall Christopher
Waller is the leading contender to
replace Fed Chair Derome Pal when his
term ends in May. He is allegedly
favored by President Donald Trump's
advisers for his forecastdriven policy
approach, a departure from the current
data dependency championed by Chair Pal.
Recall that Pal's backward-looking
approach has led to five major policy
mistakes throughout his tenure. Um, too
late to stop tightening in 2018, too
late to ease in 2020, too late to start
tightening in 2021, asleep at the bank
supervision wheel in 2023, and too late
to start easing in 2025. Sources also
site POTUS appreciates that Waller has
deep knowledge and relation deep
knowledge of and relationships within
the institution, something that would
help him implement regime change if
Waller is on board with paradigm C. Time
will tell on that.
Second, President Trump nominated
Council of Economic Advisors Chairman
Steven Moran to temporarily fill Fed
Governor Adriana Kougler's seat expiring
in January pending US Senate
confirmation. Dr. Moran, a Harvard PhD,
shares Trump's call for lower rates, is
a measured communicator, a sharp Fed
critic, and backs unconventional
reforms. With regard to the medium to
long-term outlook for monetary policy,
Moran recently stated, "Overall, we
don't expect significant inflation from
the tariffs, and if it occurs, it would
be a one-time price level shift, not an
enduring trend." A view contrasting with
uh Jerome Pal's July 30th warning that
in inflationary impacts of tariffs could
instead be more persistent. Dr. Moran's
view suggests he can inject much needed
credibility into the other side of the
debate surrounding the impact of tariffs
on inflation.
Dr. Raman represents a Trojan horse that
can help the FOMC understand the
following. Number one, tariffs are not
inflationary. Number two, a 2% inflation
target is arbitrary. And number three,
an economy growing 3 to 4% on a real
basis with 2 to 3% inflation is far
better for the median household than an
economy growing 1 to 2% because of a
firm commitment to an arbitrary 2%
inflation target. Elsewhere, President
Trump signed an executive order on
Thursday opening 401ks up to private
equity, real estate, cryptocurrency, and
other alternatives, directing the labor
market, labor department to re to
reassess their recent guidance within
six months. The 401k market represents a
massive opportunity for liquidity for
alternative asset managers given the
multi-year trend of subdued capital
markets activity. We can only hope that
Wall Street doesn't turn Main Street
into bag holders again by marketing only
the lowest quality private equity and
private credit assets to retail
investors, i.e. the assets they cannot
sell without a significant haircut.
Refer to the recent divevesture of
private equity uh assets by my alma
mater Yale and the recent debt raised by
our rivals Harvard for more details. Uh
so obviously Yale and Harvard were you
know Yale specifically but Harvard Yale
and Harvard were obviously up front in
terms of um in terms of uh you know
getting into the alternative asset class
and so uh Yale struggling to get out
without significant haircuts on on some
of its uh private portfolio and
obviously Harvard couldn't get out at
all. That's why they had to raise you
know almost a billion and a half dollars
in debt uh this past spring. So I'm
hopeful
that Wall Street doesn't turn Main
Street into bag holders by selling a
bunch of uh lowquality products uh in
this in the alternative space. But uh
you know who who's to say what's going
to happen there? But all we know is uh
I'm grateful that 42 macro exists so
that we can help Main Street navigate uh
all these pending changes not just in
the economy but also from a regulatory
standpoint so that they don't wind up
you guys don't wind up being the back
orders as uh you tend to be uh uh you
know throughout these market cycles. So
uh transitioning to our 42 macro
dashboard as always we wrap up with a
question from our community. This one's
titled stop-loss. It uh reads, "I have
the vast majority of my wealth invested
in KISS. So far so good. We've been in a
bull market since I joined 42 Macro on
June 23rd. There have been no pivots
since I joined, but I am starting to get
nervous about some sort of black swan
event like China invading Taiwan or the
bond market breaking. I really don't
want to lose my life savings due to
unforeseen circumstances. I am not a
rich man. Uh I am considered lower
middle class uh based on the cost of
living and median income in Southern
California where I reside. I don't have
any assets outside the money invested in
my brokerage account. Curious, would you
recommend that small retail clients like
myself use stop losses just in case
something crazy happens? I trust your
process, but nothing is 100%
bulletproof. I would appreciate your
thoughts on using stop losses. So, uh
first off, I just have to say uh I'm
really grateful and humbled uh that we
have people who consider themselves uh
lower middle class uh among our client
base. Uh, I'm someone who grew up at the
very bottom of the lower class of of our
K-shaped US society. Someone who spent
uh time in and out of homeless shelters,
time living in vans, time living in
motel, time sleeping on neighbors
couches. You know, I can go on and on
and on about all the hardship that I
struggled with uh throughout my
childhood. And so I'm really grateful
that uh you know I can pay it forward in
terms of the solutions that we're
building uh we have built uh uh very
effective solutions that we've built uh
for uh for retail investors around the
world and I'm really grateful to be
partnered uh with with this client here.
Uh with regards to what you should be
doing specifically and now I'm talking
to to the client that's not what we do
here at 42 macro. We don't provide
specific investment advice for
individuals. How you structure your
portfolio respond to our research is
totally up to you. All our research is
general in nature. Um, the one thing I
will say is that uh I don't know what
your stop losses are are going to be.
Not you, but if you're going to think
about putting stop losses on your
portfolio, the thing I would say is I
bet they're probably going to be worse
than our riskmanagement overlays. The
whole point of our risk management
overlays is to implement risk
management, you know, uh, signaling into
into into KISS. We we add vol targeting
with our market regime now casting
process and we add dynamic position
sizing with our volatility adjusted
momentum signal. I guarantee you our
market regime now casting process and
our volatility just momentum signal
which are based on proven quantitative
techniques from decades of research
that'll tell you that that I guarantee
you both of those uh signals are better
than your arbitrary you know lick your
finger in the air stop loss uh you know
our you know and so and and and the
reason for that is you know one of the
things that makes our models um you know
very effective and something that
obviously Wall Street has figured out a
long time ago is that
Volatility is a leading indicator for
price changes and so our volatility is
just a momentum signal which we built uh
you know kind of standing on the
shoulders of the giant Ben Mandle
broughten and his work his similar work
and the and the subject matter uh we
understand that volatility is a leading
indicator for price. So that's why we
have our volatility adjusted momentum
signal. What a stop-loss is is a pure
price momentum signal that says get me
out, sell this position perhaps at a
steep discount or panic sell the lows uh
whenever the price momentum signal uh uh
uh crosses a certain threshold. What our
volatility adjustable momentum signal
has proven across multiple market cycles
now since we started this firm, but also
in our rolling out of sample back tests
is that our Vamp signal actually causes
investors to change their positions much
faster than you otherwise would be if
you were just relying on technical
indicators or uh a price momentum signal
like a stop loss would be. So, uh,
that's a long way away of saying, yeah,
you could use a stop loss, but I'm I'm
almost certain that it's going to be a
lot worse than our institutional grade
risk management overlays. You know, we
would, most of the hedge funds on Wall
Street wouldn't be able to charge 2 and
20 and three and 30 to their clients if
their clients could just put a stop-loss
order in. You know what I mean? The
reason these people get paid to manage
market risk and market cycles and to
stay on the right side of market risk
for their clients is because they have
the ability to create tools and
solutions that are more effective than
the stuff that you can pull off the
shelf from your, you know, 1800
brokerage account. You know what I mean?
So, uh, you you can keep pulling stuff
off the shelf and dumping it in your
portfolio from your 1-800 brokerage
account, but I guarantee you're going to
have worse results than the us than me,
myself, who's using KISS to manage my
entire liquid net worth as a former dirt
poor person who grew up in standing in
food bank lines and and all kind of
stuff, eating pork and beans out of a
can, you know, in a in a in a
roachinfested motel. I got a bunch of
stories about all the hardship that I
faced and I'm telling you right now I'm
using KISS to manage my entire liquid
net worth as someone who is first
generation rich and refuses to go back
to that life lifestyle. So, uh, you guys
can obviously trust that what we built
here, uh, is, you know, very effective
and most importantly, you know,
principally designed to cut off left
tail rest from portfolios. And quite
frankly, that's, you know, I've always
believed that the markets will make the
money for you. If you're out there
trying and trading and trying to make
money in financial markets, you're doing
it wrong. The markets go up over time.
Just let it go up for you. The money is
made in the waiting, not in the trading
with that Charlie Mer Warren Buffett.
It's it's the left tail that you need to
be focused on as investors and what we
do and what we do for our institutional
clients at a high level at a very
effective level and obviously what we've
you know built for the retail investor
audience via KISS uh that stuff is in
expressly focused on risk managing the
left tail and making sure that we don't
suffer significant uh uh uh outsiz draw
downs in our portfolios. Obviously, KISS
is you know primar it is a trend
following system that's built on our
market regime now casting process and
the volatility just momentum signals for
stocks gold and bitcoin and it obviously
raises cash dynamically based on those
signals um you know when when things are
starting to get hairy um if we had some
sort of left tail like if China invaded
Taiwan right now obviously we would be
illositioned for that all investors in
the world would be ill positioned for
that so uh you know I don't live in fear
of of r of nuclear bombs going off in in
Manhattan you know like look our goal
was to take risk as financial market
participants. If we weren't taking risk,
we would not be making any money. No
one's going to get you give you money to
sit there with 100% zero% risk in your
portfolio. If you have 0% risk in your
portfolio, guess what? You're going to
make zero dollars. You have to take risk
to make money in financial markets. And
ultimately what we built here uh are
solutions that we've proven like we
talked about yesterday in our
testimonials page. Hundreds of
testimonials from clients all around the
world and 80 countries around the world
telling you that what we've built keeps
them on the right side of market risk.
It's highly effective at risk managing
bare markets and it's equally effective
at keeping them on the right side of
market risk i.e. maximizing upside
capture during uh bull markets as well.
So um that's about it. I'll wrap it up
there. Darius here presenting our macro
